sections.content: The Stone Age: Britain's First People

The first people to live in Britain were hunter-gatherers who arrived around 10,000 years ago, after the end of the last Ice Age. At that time, Britain was not yet an island but was connected to the European continent by a land bridge. These early inhabitants crossed this land bridge on foot, following herds of wild animals and foraging for food.

As the climate warmed and sea levels rose, the land bridge was gradually submerged beneath the waters of what is now the English Channel and the North Sea. Britain became an island, separated from the continent. This geographical change would profoundly shape British history, providing a natural defence against invasion.

Britain's first inhabitants arrived around 10,000 years ago as hunter-gatherers. They crossed a land bridge that connected Britain to mainland Europe, which was later submerged as sea levels rose after the Ice Age.

About 6,000 years ago, the first farmers arrived in Britain. They brought with them new skills and knowledge, including how to cultivate crops and domesticate animals. This transition from hunting and gathering to farming was one of the most significant changes in early British history. The farmers cleared forests to make fields and began to settle in permanent communities rather than moving from place to place.
Prehistoric Monuments and Early Civilisation

Stonehenge is one of the most famous prehistoric monuments in the world. Located in Wiltshire, England, it was built in several stages between about 3000 BC and 1500 BC. The massive stone circle is thought to have been used as a place of worship or a burial ground, and it may also have served as a kind of astronomical calendar. How the enormous stones were transported remains a subject of debate among historians and archaeologists.

Skara Brae, located in the Orkney Islands off the north coast of Scotland, is one of the best-preserved groups of prehistoric houses in Western Europe. Dating from around 3100 BC, this Neolithic settlement gives us a remarkable window into life in prehistoric Britain. The houses contain stone furniture including beds, dressers, and storage boxes, revealing a surprisingly sophisticated way of life.

Stonehenge was built in stages between about 3000 BC and 1500 BC in Wiltshire, England. Skara Brae in the Orkney Islands, Scotland, is one of the best-preserved prehistoric villages in Western Europe, dating from around 3100 BC.
1 ROMAN BRITAIN.png

The Bronze Age began in Britain around 2000 BC, when people learned to make tools and weapons from bronze, an alloy of copper and tin. This represented a major technological advance over the stone tools used previously. Bronze Age people were skilled metalworkers and also created elaborate jewellery and ceremonial objects.

The Iron Age followed, beginning around 800 BC. Iron was stronger than bronze and could be used to make more effective tools and weapons. During the Iron Age, Celtic tribes from central Europe began to settle in Britain, bringing their distinctive language and culture with them.
The Celtic Peoples

The Celts were not a single unified people but a collection of tribes who shared similar languages, religious beliefs, and artistic traditions. They were skilled warriors who built impressive hill forts across Britain for defence, such as Maiden Castle in Dorset. Celtic society was organised into tribal groups, each led by a chieftain.

The Celtic languages they brought with them survive to this day. Welsh is spoken widely in Wales, and Scottish Gaelic and Irish Gaelic are still spoken in parts of Scotland and Northern Ireland respectively. These living languages are among the oldest in Europe and represent a direct link to Britain's ancient past.

Celtic tribes settled in Britain during the Iron Age (from about 800 BC). Their languages survive today as Welsh, spoken in Wales, and Gaelic, spoken in parts of Scotland and Ireland. The Celts built hill forts for defence and were skilled in metalwork and art.
Key periods of early British prehistory:
items: Around 10,000 years ago: First hunter-gatherers arrived via a land bridge from continental Europe
About 6,000 years ago: First farmers arrived, beginning the transition to settled agriculture
3000-1500 BC: Stonehenge was built in stages in Wiltshire, England
Around 3100 BC: Skara Brae settlement in the Orkney Islands was inhabited
Around 2000 BC: The Bronze Age began with the use of bronze tools and weapons
Around 800 BC: The Iron Age began; Celtic tribes settled in Britain bringing their languages and culture

sections.content: The Roman Invasion and Occupation
11 ROMAN INVASION.png

The Roman general Julius Caesar led two expeditions to Britain in 55 BC and 54 BC. Although his forces landed and fought some battles, these expeditions were not full-scale invasions and he did not establish permanent control. It was nearly a century later, in AD 43, that the Emperor Claudius launched a successful invasion of Britain with a large army of about 40,000 soldiers.

The Romans quickly conquered much of what is now England and Wales, and they remained in Britain for nearly 400 years. However, they never fully conquered Scotland or Ireland. To mark the northern boundary of the Roman Empire in Britain, Emperor Hadrian ordered the construction of a great wall. Hadrian's Wall, stretching 73 miles from coast to coast across the north of England, was built to keep out the Picts, the ancestors of the Scottish people.

Julius Caesar first attempted to invade Britain in 55 BC, but the successful Roman invasion came in AD 43 under Emperor Claudius. Hadrian's Wall was built on the orders of Emperor Hadrian to mark the northern frontier of the Roman Empire and to keep out the Picts.

One of the most dramatic episodes of Roman Britain was the revolt led by Boudicca (also known as Boadicea), queen of the Iceni tribe in eastern England. In AD 60 or 61, angered by Roman mistreatment, Boudicca led a major uprising against the Roman occupiers. Her forces destroyed the Roman towns of Colchester, St Albans, and London before being defeated. Boudicca remains a symbol of British resistance to this day.
The Legacy of Roman Britain

The Romans made an enormous impact on Britain. They built a network of straight roads, many of which still form the basis of major routes used today. They founded towns and cities, including London (Londinium), Bath (Aquae Sulis), and Chester (Deva). They also introduced public baths, central heating systems, aqueducts, and impressive public buildings.

During the Roman period, Christianity was introduced to Britain for the first time. Although it did not immediately replace the existing Celtic and Roman religions, the new faith gradually gained followers. By the time the Romans left Britain, Christianity had become an established religion among some communities.

Boudicca, queen of the Iceni tribe, led a famous revolt against the Romans in AD 60-61, destroying the towns of Colchester, London, and St Albans before being defeated. She remains an iconic figure in British history.

The Romans eventually left Britain around AD 410. The Roman Empire was under attack from many different peoples across Europe, and the Roman army was needed to defend Rome itself. When they withdrew, Britain was left without the organised military defence and administrative structure that had maintained order for nearly four centuries. This created a power vacuum that would soon be filled by new invaders from across the North Sea.

The Romans left Britain around AD 410 to defend other parts of the Roman Empire. They left behind roads, towns, public baths, and the foundations of Christianity. Roman rule in Britain lasted nearly 400 years.
Key features and legacy of Roman Britain:
items: Hadrian's Wall was built to keep out the Picts and stretched 73 miles across northern England
The Romans built a network of roads, many of which still form the basis of modern routes
They founded major towns including London (Londinium) and Bath (Aquae Sulis)
Public baths, central heating, and aqueducts were introduced
Christianity was introduced to Britain during the Roman occupation
Roman rule lasted from AD 43 until around AD 410, when the army withdrew

items: 10,000 years ago: First hunter-gatherers arrive in Britain
6,000 years ago: First farmers arrive
3000-1500 BC: Stonehenge built
2000 BC: Bronze Age begins
800 BC: Iron Age begins; Celtic tribes settle
55 BC: Julius Caesar's first expedition to Britain
AD 43: Emperor Claudius's successful invasion
AD 60-61: Boudicca's revolt against the Romans
AD 410: Romans leave Britain

sections.content: The Arrival of the Anglo-Saxons
2 ANGLO-SAXON BRITAIN.png

After the Romans left Britain around AD 410, the country was vulnerable to invasion. Tribes from northern Europe began to invade and settle in Britain. The Jutes came from the area that is now the Jutland Peninsula in Denmark, the Angles came from what is now Schleswig-Holstein in northern Germany, and the Saxons came from the lowlands of northern Germany. These groups are collectively known as the Anglo-Saxons.

The Anglo-Saxons gradually pushed the native Celtic Britons westward into Wales, Cornwall, and Scotland. They established several separate kingdoms across England, the most important of which were Northumbria in the north, Mercia in the Midlands, East Anglia in the east, and Wessex in the south and west. The name 'England' derives from 'Angle-land', the land of the Angles.

The Jutes, Angles, and Saxons invaded Britain from Denmark and Germany after the Romans left in AD 410. The Angles gave their name to 'Angle-land', which became 'England'. They established several kingdoms including Northumbria, Mercia, East Anglia, and Wessex.

The Anglo-Saxons were not Christians when they first arrived in Britain. They worshipped their own gods, including Woden and Thor, whose names survive in our days of the week (Wednesday and Thursday). The conversion to Christianity was a gradual process that transformed Anglo-Saxon society, culture, and learning.
The Conversion to Christianity

In AD 597, Pope Gregory the Great sent a mission to Britain led by St Augustine to convert the Anglo-Saxons to Christianity. St Augustine arrived in Kent and was welcomed by King Ethelbert, whose wife was already a Christian. Augustine successfully converted Ethelbert and established a church at Canterbury, becoming the first Archbishop of Canterbury. The position of Archbishop of Canterbury remains the most senior role in the Church of England to this day.

St Augustine was sent by Pope Gregory the Great to convert the Anglo-Saxons to Christianity in AD 597. He became the first Archbishop of Canterbury after converting King Ethelbert of Kent. Canterbury has remained the centre of the Church of England ever since.

Christianity brought literacy, learning, and new forms of art to Anglo-Saxon England. Monasteries became centres of education and culture, producing illuminated manuscripts and preserving ancient knowledge. The monks kept written records that are among our most important sources for understanding early English history.
Anglo-Saxon Culture and Governance

The Anglo-Saxons produced one of the greatest works of early English literature: the epic poem Beowulf. Written in Old English, it tells the story of a Scandinavian warrior who battles monsters and a dragon. It is the longest surviving Anglo-Saxon poem and provides valuable insights into the values and beliefs of the time, including honour, loyalty, and courage.

The Sutton Hoo treasure, discovered in Suffolk in 1939, is one of the most spectacular archaeological finds in British history. It was the burial site of an Anglo-Saxon king, probably Raedwald of East Anglia, who died around AD 625. The treasure included a magnificent ship burial containing a golden helmet, elaborate jewellery, weapons, and precious objects from across Europe, revealing the wealth and sophistication of Anglo-Saxon civilisation.

Beowulf is the most famous Anglo-Saxon poem, written in Old English, telling the story of a hero who fights monsters. The Sutton Hoo treasure, discovered in 1939 in Suffolk, revealed the richness of Anglo-Saxon culture through a magnificent ship burial.

The Anglo-Saxons developed an important system of governance. The Witan (also called the Witenagemot) was a council of wise men, typically made up of noblemen, bishops, and other senior figures, who advised the king on important matters including laws, taxation, and even the succession to the throne. The Witan can be seen as an early forerunner of the modern Parliament.
3 VIKING AGE.png
The Viking Invasions and the Danelaw
12 VIKINGS RAID LINISDARNE.png

The Vikings first raided the coast of Britain in AD 789, attacking the monastery at Lindisfarne in AD 793 in one of the most famous early raids. The Vikings came from Denmark and Norway, and they were fearsome warriors and skilled seafarers. Initially they came to raid and plunder, targeting the rich monasteries along the coast, but gradually they began to settle permanently in Britain.

The Vikings conquered large parts of eastern and northern England, establishing an area known as the Danelaw, where Danish law and customs prevailed. Many English place names, especially in the north and east, have Scandinavian origins, ending in '-by' (meaning village, as in Whitby and Derby) or '-thorpe' (meaning settlement, as in Cleethorpes). The Vikings also had a significant impact on the English language, contributing many everyday words.

Viking raids began in AD 789, with the famous attack on Lindisfarne monastery in AD 793. The Vikings established the Danelaw in eastern and northern England, where Danish law prevailed. Many English place names have Viking origins, such as those ending in '-by' and '-thorpe'.

King Alfred the Great of Wessex (reigned 871-899) is one of the most important figures in English history. He is the only English king to be given the title 'the Great'. Alfred fought a long struggle against the Vikings and eventually defeated them at the Battle of Edington in 878, forcing the Viking leader Guthrum to accept baptism as a Christian and to withdraw to the Danelaw.

Alfred was not only a great military leader but also a patron of learning and education. He promoted the use of English rather than Latin in education and government, and he commissioned the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle, a historical record of events in England that was continued for centuries after his death. Alfred also reformed the legal system and built a network of fortified towns called burhs for defence against the Vikings.

King Alfred the Great of Wessex is the only English king to be called 'the Great'. He defeated the Vikings at the Battle of Edington in 878, promoted education and learning, and commissioned the Anglo-Saxon Chronicle.
The Road to 1066

Alfred's successors continued his work of unifying the English kingdoms. His grandson, Athelstan, became the first king to rule over all of England when he conquered the last Viking strongholds in the north in AD 927. Athelstan is therefore regarded as the first true king of all England, and his achievement laid the foundations for the unified English kingdom.

In 1042, Edward the Confessor became king. He was a deeply religious man who is best remembered for ordering the building of Westminster Abbey, where he was buried in 1066. Edward died on 5 January 1066 without a clear heir, leading to a succession crisis. Harold Godwinson, the powerful Earl of Wessex, was chosen by the Witan as the new king and became Harold II, but his claim was immediately challenged by Harald Hardrada, King of Norway, and William, Duke of Normandy.

Athelstan, grandson of Alfred the Great, became the first king of all England in AD 927. Edward the Confessor built Westminster Abbey and died in 1066 without a clear heir. Harold II, the last Anglo-Saxon king, was defeated and killed at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066.

The Battle of Hastings took place on 14 October 1066. Harold II was killed during the battle, traditionally said to have been shot in the eye with an arrow, though historians debate this. His defeat and death marked the end of Anglo-Saxon England and the beginning of a new era under Norman rule. Harold II was the last Anglo-Saxon king of England.
Key Anglo-Saxon contributions to English history:
items: The Anglo-Saxons gave England its name (from 'Angle-land')
St Augustine established Christianity at Canterbury in AD 597
Beowulf is the greatest surviving work of Anglo-Saxon literature
The Sutton Hoo treasure revealed the sophistication of Anglo-Saxon culture
The Witan was an early form of advisory council, a precursor to Parliament
King Alfred the Great defeated the Vikings and promoted learning and education
The Anglo-Saxon Chronicle is one of the most important historical records of early England

sections.content: Important dates for the Anglo-Saxon and Viking period:
items: AD 410: Romans leave Britain; Anglo-Saxon invasions begin
AD 597: St Augustine arrives in Kent; becomes first Archbishop of Canterbury
AD 789: First Viking raids on the British coast
AD 793: Vikings attack Lindisfarne monastery
AD 878: Alfred the Great defeats the Vikings at the Battle of Edington
AD 927: Athelstan becomes first king of all England
1042: Edward the Confessor becomes king
1066: Harold II defeated at the Battle of Hastings; end of Anglo-Saxon England

title: The Norman Conquest and Middle Ages

sections.content: 1066: The Norman Conquest
13 BATTLE OF HASTINGS.png

William, Duke of Normandy, defeated King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066. Harold was killed during the battle, and William marched his army to London. He was crowned King of England on Christmas Day 1066 at Westminster Abbey, the church that Edward the Confessor had built. The Norman Conquest was the last successful foreign invasion of England.

William the Conqueror immediately began to consolidate his power. He faced several rebellions, particularly in the north of England, which he crushed ruthlessly in a campaign known as the 'Harrying of the North' (1069-1070). He replaced most of the Anglo-Saxon nobility with Norman lords and introduced the feudal system, in which all land belonged to the king and was granted to his followers in exchange for military service.

William the Conqueror won the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066 and was crowned King of England on Christmas Day 1066 at Westminster Abbey. This was the last successful foreign invasion of England.

In 1086, William commissioned the Domesday Book, a comprehensive survey of all the land and property in England. It recorded who owned the land, how much it was worth, and what resources were available, including livestock, ploughs, and woodland. The Domesday Book was used to determine how much tax people owed to the king. It still exists today and is kept at the National Archives in Kew.
15 MAGNA CARTA.png

The Domesday Book was commissioned by William the Conqueror in 1086. It was a detailed survey of all land and property in England, used to calculate taxes. It still survives and is kept at the National Archives.
Norman England: Castles, Language, and the Feudal System

The Normans built castles across England to maintain control over the conquered population. The most famous of these is the Tower of London, begun by William the Conqueror in 1066, which has served over the centuries as a royal palace, a prison, a treasury, and a place of execution. Windsor Castle, also built during William's reign, is the oldest and largest occupied castle in the world and remains a royal residence.

The Norman Conquest had a profound effect on the English language. The Normans spoke a form of French, and for centuries French was the language of the royal court, the law, and the aristocracy, while ordinary people continued to speak English. Over time, the two languages blended together, which is why modern English contains so many words derived from French, particularly in the areas of government, law, food, and culture.

The Normans built important castles including the Tower of London and Windsor Castle. Norman French influenced the English language enormously, contributing many words still used in law, government, and everyday speech.

The feudal system was the foundation of Norman society. The king owned all the land and granted large estates (fiefs) to his most loyal supporters, the barons and bishops, in return for military service and loyalty. These lords in turn granted land to lesser nobles and knights, who provided soldiers. At the bottom of the hierarchy were the peasants, or serfs, who worked the land and owed service to their lord.
Magna Carta and the Origins of Parliament

Magna Carta (the Great Charter) was sealed by King John at Runnymede, near Windsor, on 15 June 1215. King John had angered his barons through heavy taxation and abuse of power, and they forced him to agree to a charter that limited the power of the monarch. Magna Carta established the fundamental principle that even the king was subject to the law, and it guaranteed certain rights including the right to a fair trial. It is considered one of the most important documents in the history of democracy and constitutional government, influencing the United States Constitution and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights.

Magna Carta was signed by King John at Runnymede in 1215. It established the principle that even the king was subject to the law and is considered one of the most important documents in constitutional history.

The origins of Parliament can be traced to this period. In 1264, Simon de Montfort, Earl of Leicester, rebelled against King Henry III and called a parliament that included, for the first time, not only nobles and clergy but also representatives of the common people from towns and boroughs. Although de Montfort was killed in battle the following year, his innovation survived. This is often regarded as the beginning of the English Parliament as a representative body.

Simon de Montfort called the first English Parliament that included common people in 1264, during a rebellion against King Henry III. This is seen as the origin of representative parliamentary government in England.
Wales, Scotland, and the Hundred Years War

King Edward I conquered Wales in the 1280s and imposed English law through the Statute of Rhuddlan in 1284. He built a ring of magnificent castles, including Caernarfon, Conwy, and Harlech, to maintain English control. Edward named his son the Prince of Wales, a title that has been given to the eldest son of the English (later British) monarch ever since.

Scotland resisted English domination. Robert the Bruce, having claimed the Scottish throne in 1306, led Scotland to a decisive victory over the English at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314. This battle secured Scottish independence, which was formally recognised by the Treaty of Edinburgh-Northampton in 1328. Robert the Bruce is regarded as one of Scotland's greatest national heroes.
Edward I conquered Wales and imposed English law through the Statute of Rhuddlan (1284). Robert the Bruce won Scottish independence by defeating the English at the Battle of Bannockburn in 1314.

The Hundred Years War (1337-1453) was a long series of conflicts between England and France over the English claim to the French throne. One of the most famous English victories was the Battle of Agincourt in 1415, where King Henry V defeated a much larger French army. However, by 1453, England had lost almost all its territory in France, retaining only the port of Calais.
The Black Death, Peasants' Revolt, and the Wars of the Roses
16 BLACK DEATH.png

The Black Death, a devastating plague, reached England in 1348. It was carried by fleas on rats and spread rapidly through the population. The disease killed approximately one third of the population of England. The huge loss of life had far-reaching social and economic consequences: with fewer workers available, those who survived could demand higher wages, and the rigid feudal system began to break down.

The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was a major uprising triggered by a new poll tax and widespread discontent among the lower classes. Led by Wat Tyler, thousands of peasants marched on London, demanding an end to serfdom and unfair taxation. Although the revolt was eventually suppressed and Tyler was killed, it demonstrated the growing power and discontent of the common people and contributed to the eventual end of serfdom in England.
The Black Death reached England in 1348 and killed approximately one third of the population. The Peasants' Revolt of 1381, led by Wat Tyler, was a major uprising against unfair taxes and serfdom.

The Wars of the Roses (1455-1485) were a series of civil wars fought between two rival branches of the royal family: the House of Lancaster, whose symbol was a red rose, and the House of York, whose symbol was a white rose. The wars ended in 1485 when Henry Tudor, a Lancastrian, defeated King Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth Field and became King Henry VII, founding the Tudor dynasty.

Geoffrey Chaucer (c. 1343-1400) is often called the father of English literature. His most famous work, The Canterbury Tales, is a collection of stories told by a group of pilgrims travelling from London to the shrine of Thomas Becket at Canterbury. Written in Middle English, it provides a vivid and often humorous portrait of medieval English society. England's wool trade was a major source of wealth during the Middle Ages, and the export of wool to Europe made England one of the richest countries in the medieval world.

The Wars of the Roses (1455-1485) were fought between the Houses of Lancaster (red rose) and York (white rose). They ended with Henry Tudor's victory at the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. Geoffrey Chaucer wrote The Canterbury Tales, one of the greatest works of medieval English literature.
Key developments of the Norman and Medieval period:
items: The feudal system organised society with the king at the top, followed by barons, knights, and peasants
Norman castles including the Tower of London and Windsor Castle were built to maintain control
Norman French blended with English, greatly enriching the English language
Magna Carta (1215) established the principle that the king is subject to the law
The first representative Parliament was called by Simon de Montfort in 1264
Great medieval cathedrals were built across England, many of which still stand
The English wool trade was a major source of national wealth

sections.content: The Tudor Dynasty (1485-1603)

The Tudor period began when Henry Tudor defeated King Richard III at the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485. Henry became King Henry VII and united the warring houses of Lancaster and York by marrying Elizabeth of York. The Tudor rose, which combined the red rose of Lancaster and the white rose of York, became the symbol of the new dynasty. Henry VII was a shrewd and careful ruler who restored stability and strengthened the finances of the crown.

Henry VIII, who reigned from 1509 to 1547, is one of the most famous monarchs in English history. He is best known for having six wives and for breaking with the Catholic Church to establish the Church of England. Henry wanted to divorce his first wife, Catherine of Aragon, because she had not given him a male heir, but the Pope refused to grant an annulment. In response, Henry declared himself the Supreme Head of the Church of England through the Act of Supremacy in 1534.

Henry VII won the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485, ending the Wars of the Roses and founding the Tudor dynasty. Henry VIII broke with Rome and established the Church of England in 1534 through the Act of Supremacy, making himself Supreme Head of the Church.
6 TUDOR ENGLAND.png

Henry VIII's six wives were: Catherine of Aragon (divorced), Anne Boleyn (executed), Jane Seymour (died after childbirth), Anne of Cleves (divorced), Catherine Howard (executed), and Catherine Parr (survived him). Jane Seymour gave Henry his longed-for male heir, the future Edward VI. The mnemonic 'divorced, beheaded, died, divorced, beheaded, survived' is often used to remember the fates of his six wives.

After breaking with Rome, Henry VIII ordered the dissolution of the monasteries between 1536 and 1541. The wealth of the monasteries was seized by the crown and their lands were sold to the nobility and gentry. This was one of the largest redistributions of land and wealth in English history and created a new class of landowners who had a vested interest in supporting the Protestant settlement.

Henry VIII had six wives: Catherine of Aragon, Anne Boleyn, Jane Seymour, Anne of Cleves, Catherine Howard, and Catherine Parr. He dissolved the monasteries between 1536 and 1541, seizing their vast wealth and lands for the crown.
henry-viii-wives.png
Edward VI, Mary I, and Elizabeth I

Edward VI became king at just nine years old in 1547 and reigned until his death in 1553 at the age of 15. Although his reign was short, it saw significant Protestant reforms to the Church of England. The Book of Common Prayer was introduced, establishing a standard form of worship in English rather than Latin.

Mary I, daughter of Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon, came to the throne in 1553 and attempted to restore Catholicism to England. She reversed Edward's Protestant reforms and ordered the burning of nearly 300 Protestants at the stake, earning her the nickname 'Bloody Mary'. She married King Philip II of Spain, but the marriage was unpopular in England. Mary died in 1558 without an heir.

Edward VI (1547-1553) introduced Protestant reforms including the Book of Common Prayer. Mary I (1553-1558) tried to restore Catholicism and was known as 'Bloody Mary' for burning nearly 300 Protestants. Both died without heirs.

Elizabeth I, daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn, became queen in 1558 and reigned until 1603. Her long reign is known as the Elizabethan era and is often regarded as a Golden Age of English history. Elizabeth established a moderate Protestant settlement for the Church of England. She never married and was known as the 'Virgin Queen', and the colony of Virginia in America was named in her honour.

Elizabeth's reign saw a remarkable flowering of English culture, literature, and exploration. William Shakespeare (1564-1616), the greatest writer in the English language, wrote many of his plays during Elizabeth's reign, performing at her court and at the Globe Theatre in London.

In 1588, King Philip II of Spain sent a great fleet called the Spanish Armada to invade England. The English navy, aided by smaller, faster ships and severe storms, defeated the Armada in one of the most famous military victories in English history. Elizabeth's reign also saw the voyages of Sir Francis Drake, who circumnavigated the globe, and Sir Walter Raleigh, who attempted to establish English colonies in North America.

Elizabeth I reigned from 1558 to 1603 in a period known as the Golden Age. She was called the 'Virgin Queen' because she never married. The Spanish Armada was defeated in 1588. Shakespeare, Drake, and Raleigh all flourished during her reign.
19 SPANISH ARMADA.png
The Early Stuarts and the Civil War
7 STUART BRITAIN.png

When Elizabeth I died in 1603 without an heir, the crown passed to King James VI of Scotland, who became King James I of England. This was the union of the crowns, bringing England and Scotland under one monarch for the first time, though they remained separate kingdoms with separate parliaments. James commissioned a new English translation of the Bible, known as the King James Bible (1611), which had an enormous influence on the English language.

In 1605, a group of Catholic conspirators led by Guy Fawkes attempted to blow up the Houses of Parliament during the State Opening, when King James I and the entire Parliament would be present. The plot was discovered on 5 November when Fawkes was found in the cellars beneath Parliament guarding barrels of gunpowder. The Gunpowder Plot is commemorated every year on 5 November, known as Bonfire Night or Guy Fawkes Night, with fireworks and bonfires.

James VI of Scotland became James I of England in 1603, uniting the crowns. He commissioned the King James Bible (1611). The Gunpowder Plot of 1605, led by Guy Fawkes, was a failed Catholic conspiracy to blow up Parliament, commemorated annually on 5 November.

Charles I, who succeeded James I in 1625, believed strongly in the divine right of kings and clashed repeatedly with Parliament over taxation and religious policy. These tensions led to the English Civil War (1642-1651), fought between the Royalists (Cavaliers) and the Parliamentarians (Roundheads) led by Oliver Cromwell. Charles I was defeated, put on trial for treason, and executed on 30 January 1649 outside the Banqueting House in Whitehall. England was declared a republic, known as the Commonwealth.

The English Civil War (1642-1651) was fought between the Royalists (Cavaliers) supporting Charles I and the Parliamentarians (Roundheads) led by Oliver Cromwell. Charles I was executed in 1649, and England became a republic called the Commonwealth.
The Commonwealth, Restoration, and Glorious Revolution

Oliver Cromwell became Lord Protector of the Commonwealth in 1653, effectively ruling as a military dictator until his death in 1658. He imposed strict Puritan values on the country, banning Christmas celebrations, theatres, and other forms of entertainment. Cromwell also led a brutal military campaign in Ireland that is still remembered with bitterness. After his death, his son Richard briefly succeeded him but lacked his father's authority.

In 1660, the monarchy was restored when Charles II, the eldest son of Charles I, was invited back from exile to become king. This period is known as the Restoration. Charles II was a popular monarch who reopened the theatres and brought a more relaxed atmosphere after the austerity of the Cromwell years. However, his reign was marked by two great disasters: the Great Plague of 1665, which killed around 100,000 people in London, and the Great Fire of London in 1666, which destroyed much of the city.

Oliver Cromwell ruled as Lord Protector during the Commonwealth. The Restoration of the monarchy brought Charles II to the throne in 1660. The Great Plague (1665) killed about 100,000 in London, and the Great Fire of London (1666) destroyed much of the city.

The Glorious Revolution of 1688 occurred when Protestant lords invited William of Orange, a Dutch prince married to Mary (daughter of James II), to take the throne from the Catholic James II. James fled to France, and William and Mary became joint monarchs. In 1689, Parliament passed the Bill of Rights, confirming that the monarch could not raise taxes or maintain a standing army without Parliament's consent, guaranteeing free elections and freedom of speech in Parliament, and establishing that no Catholic could become king or queen.

The Glorious Revolution (1688) replaced the Catholic James II with the Protestant William and Mary. The Bill of Rights (1689) confirmed Parliament's supremacy over the monarch, guaranteed free elections, and established key constitutional principles still in force today.
Henry VIII's six wives and their fates:
items: Catherine of Aragon - Divorced (mother of Mary I)
Anne Boleyn - Executed (mother of Elizabeth I)
Jane Seymour - Died after giving birth to Edward VI
Anne of Cleves - Divorced (marriage annulled after six months)
Catherine Howard - Executed (accused of adultery)
Catherine Parr - Survived Henry (outlived him)

items: William Shakespeare (1564-1616): Greatest writer in the English language; wrote plays including Hamlet, Romeo and Juliet, and A Midsummer Night's Dream
Sir Francis Drake: First Englishman to circumnavigate the globe; helped defeat the Spanish Armada
Sir Walter Raleigh: Explorer who attempted to establish English colonies in North America
Elizabeth I: The 'Virgin Queen' who presided over England's Golden Age

sections.content: The Act of Union and the Enlightenment

The Act of Union of 1707 united the kingdoms of England and Scotland, creating the Kingdom of Great Britain with a single Parliament at Westminster. Scotland kept its own legal system, education system, and the Presbyterian Church of Scotland as the established church. The Hanoverian succession began in 1714 when George I became king, and Sir Robert Walpole is generally regarded as Britain's first Prime Minister, serving from 1721 to 1742.

The Act of Union 1707 united England and Scotland into the Kingdom of Great Britain. Scotland retained its own legal and education systems. The Hanoverian succession began in 1714 with George I, and Sir Robert Walpole became the first Prime Minister in 1721.

The 18th century was the age of the Enlightenment, a period of great intellectual and scientific progress. In Scotland, the Scottish Enlightenment produced some of the most influential thinkers in history. Adam Smith published The Wealth of Nations in 1776, laying the foundations of modern economics and arguing for free trade and the power of markets. David Hume made major contributions to philosophy, and James Watt's improvements to the steam engine helped launch the Industrial Revolution.
The Industrial Revolution
8 INDUSTRIAL BRITAIN.png

The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain in the late 18th century, was one of the most transformative periods in human history. It saw the shift from a largely agricultural economy to one based on manufacturing and industry. Britain was the first country to industrialise, earning the title 'the workshop of the world'. Key inventions included James Watt's improved steam engine, James Hargreaves' spinning jenny, and Richard Arkwright's water frame.

The growth of industry led to rapid urbanisation, as people moved from the countryside to work in factories in cities such as Manchester, Birmingham, and Glasgow. Living and working conditions were often appalling, with long hours, child labour, and overcrowding. New transport networks were essential: canals carried heavy goods, and the railways, pioneered by George Stephenson and Isambard Kingdom Brunel, transformed travel from the 1830s onwards.

The Industrial Revolution began in Britain in the late 18th century. Key innovations included James Watt's steam engine and Hargreaves' spinning jenny. Britain became known as 'the workshop of the world'. Canals and later railways were built to transport goods and people.

The agricultural revolution went hand in hand with industrialisation. New farming techniques, crop rotation methods, and selective breeding of livestock greatly increased food production. However, the enclosure of common land forced many rural people off the land and into the cities to find work, fuelling the growth of the industrial workforce.
The British Empire and the Abolition of Slavery

The British Empire grew significantly during the 18th and 19th centuries, eventually becoming the largest empire in history, covering about a quarter of the world's land surface. Britain also played a major role in the transatlantic slave trade, but a powerful abolition movement led by William Wilberforce, Olaudah Equiano, and Granville Sharp campaigned to end this cruel practice. The slave trade was abolished in the British Empire in 1807, and slavery itself was abolished throughout most of the Empire in 1833.

The British Empire became the largest in history, covering about a quarter of the world's land surface. William Wilberforce led the campaign to abolish slavery: the slave trade was abolished in 1807, and slavery itself was abolished in 1833.

The Crimean War (1853-1856), fought against Russia, is notable for the work of Florence Nightingale. She led a team of nurses caring for wounded British soldiers at the military hospital in Scutari, Turkey. Her insistence on hygiene and sanitation dramatically reduced the death rate among patients and laid the foundations for modern nursing. She later established the first professional nursing school at St Thomas' Hospital in London.
The Victorian Era, Women's Suffrage, and the World Wars
9 WORLD WAR BRITAIN.png

Queen Victoria reigned from 1837 to 1901, presiding over a period of enormous change, industrial growth, and imperial expansion. The Great Exhibition of 1851, held in the Crystal Palace in Hyde Park, London, showcased Britain's achievements to the world. During Victoria's reign, the Irish potato famine (1845-1852) killed about one million people and caused a further million to emigrate, deepening Irish resentment of British rule.

Queen Victoria reigned from 1837 to 1901. The Great Exhibition of 1851 celebrated British industrial achievement. The Irish potato famine (1845-1852) killed about one million people and caused a million more to emigrate.

The campaign for women's suffrage gained momentum in the early 20th century. The suffragettes, led by Emmeline Pankhurst and her daughters Christabel and Sylvia, used militant tactics including chaining themselves to railings, smashing windows, and going on hunger strikes to demand women's right to vote. In 1918, women over the age of 30 gained the right to vote. Full equal voting rights for women, at the same age as men (21), were achieved in 1928.
Emmeline Pankhurst led the suffragettes in the campaign for women's right to vote. Women over 30 gained the vote in 1918, and equal voting rights at age 21 were achieved in 1928.

The First World War (1914-1918) was a devastating global conflict. Britain and its allies (including France, Russia, and later the United States) fought against Germany and the Central Powers. The war was characterised by trench warfare on the Western Front, with enormous casualties on both sides. Over 2 million British military casualties were sustained. The war led to significant social changes, including greater roles for women in the workforce.

The Second World War (1939-1945) began when Nazi Germany invaded Poland. After the fall of France in 1940, Britain stood alone against Nazi Germany. Winston Churchill became Prime Minister in May 1940 and rallied the nation with his inspirational speeches. The Battle of Britain (1940) was a crucial air battle in which the RAF prevented a German invasion. D-Day, 6 June 1944, saw the Allied invasion of Normandy, the largest seaborne invasion in history, beginning the liberation of Western Europe.

Winston Churchill led Britain through most of World War II (1940-1945). The Battle of Britain (1940) prevented a German invasion. D-Day (6 June 1944) was the Allied invasion of Normandy. Churchill was voted the greatest Briton of all time in a 2002 BBC poll.
Post-War Britain to the Present Day
10 MODERN BRITAIN.png

After the war, Clement Attlee's Labour government (elected in 1945) created the welfare state based on the Beveridge Report of 1942, and established the National Health Service (NHS) in 1948, providing free healthcare for all. Britain's colonies gradually gained independence in a process known as decolonisation, with India and Pakistan gaining independence in 1947. Many former colonies joined the Commonwealth of Nations.

Clement Attlee's Labour government established the NHS in 1948 and created the welfare state based on the Beveridge Report (1942). Decolonisation after WWII saw former colonies gain independence, many joining the Commonwealth of Nations.

The 1960s, known as the 'Swinging Sixties', was a period of cultural revolution in Britain. London became a centre of fashion, music, and youth culture. The Beatles, the Rolling Stones, and other British bands led a musical revolution that influenced popular culture around the world. Social attitudes became more liberal, with important legislation on issues such as the abolition of the death penalty (1965), the decriminalisation of homosexuality (1967), and the legalisation of abortion (1967).

The Troubles in Northern Ireland was a violent conflict from the late 1960s to 1998 between nationalists (mostly Catholic) and unionists (mostly Protestant), claiming over 3,000 lives before being ended by the Good Friday Agreement of 1998. Margaret Thatcher, Prime Minister from 1979 to 1990, was the first woman to hold the office, and she transformed British politics by reducing trade union power, privatising state-owned industries, and promoting free-market economics.

The Good Friday Agreement (1998) brought peace to Northern Ireland after decades of conflict known as the Troubles. Margaret Thatcher (Prime Minister 1979-1990) was Britain's first female Prime Minister.

Devolution in the late 1990s gave Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland their own elected assemblies. The Scottish Parliament and the National Assembly for Wales (now Senedd Cymru/Welsh Parliament) were established in 1999. Britain joined the European Economic Community (EEC) in 1973, but in a referendum on 23 June 2016, the UK voted to leave the European Union (Brexit), formally leaving on 31 January 2020. The COVID-19 pandemic beginning in 2020 had a profound impact, with lockdowns imposed and the NHS under enormous pressure, though Britain played a leading role in vaccine development.
Key developments of modern Britain:
items: Act of Union 1707: United England and Scotland into Great Britain
Industrial Revolution (late 18th century): Transformed Britain from agricultural to industrial society
Abolition of slave trade (1807) and slavery (1833): Led by William Wilberforce
Victorian era (1837-1901): Period of imperial expansion and industrial growth
Women's suffrage: Women over 30 gained the vote in 1918; equal voting rights in 1928
NHS established in 1948: Free healthcare at the point of use for all
Devolution (1999): Scottish Parliament and Welsh Assembly established
Brexit referendum (2016): UK voted to leave the European Union

sections.content: The Palace of Westminster and Parliamentary Sovereignty

The UK Parliament is located at the Palace of Westminster in London, one of the most iconic buildings in the world. The palace sits on the north bank of the River Thames and has been the seat of English and British government for centuries. The current building was largely rebuilt after a fire in 1834, designed in the Gothic Revival style by architect Charles Barry with interiors by Augustus Pugin.

Parliamentary sovereignty is a fundamental principle of the UK constitution. It means that Parliament is the supreme legal authority in the UK and can create or end any law. No body, including the courts, can override or set aside legislation passed by Parliament. This principle distinguishes the UK from countries with codified constitutions where a constitutional court can strike down laws.

The UK Parliament is a bicameral legislature, meaning it is made up of two chambers: the House of Commons and the House of Lords. Both chambers are involved in the process of making laws and scrutinising the work of the government, but the House of Commons is the more powerful of the two.
The House of Commons

The House of Commons is the democratically elected lower chamber of Parliament. It has 650 Members of Parliament (MPs), each representing a geographical area known as a constituency. The UK is divided into 650 constituencies, each with roughly similar population sizes. Every constituency elects one MP to represent them in Parliament through a voting system called first-past-the-post, where the candidate with the most votes wins the seat.

The political party that wins the most seats in a general election usually forms the government. The leader of that party becomes the Prime Minister. If no single party wins a majority of seats (more than 325), the result is called a hung parliament, which may lead to a coalition government or a minority government. The second largest party in the House of Commons becomes the Official Opposition.

The Speaker of the House of Commons is an MP elected by other MPs to preside over debates. The Speaker must be politically neutral and does not vote on legislation, except to break a tie. Once elected, the Speaker gives up all party political affiliations. The Speaker ensures that the rules of the House are followed and that debates are conducted fairly.

The Prime Minister is the head of government and lives at 10 Downing Street in London, which also serves as the PM's office. The Prime Minister chairs Cabinet meetings, appoints and dismisses ministers, represents the UK internationally, and is responsible for policy and decision-making. The PM must be a Member of Parliament and must retain the confidence of the House of Commons to stay in power.
The House of Lords

The House of Lords is the upper chamber of Parliament. Unlike the House of Commons, members of the House of Lords are not elected by the public. The House of Lords includes life peers, who are appointed by the monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister and hold their titles for life only. It also includes a small number of hereditary peers, who have inherited their titles, and bishops of the Church of England known as the Lords Spiritual.

The main role of the House of Lords is to scrutinise bills that have been passed by the House of Commons, suggest amendments, and provide expert debate on public policy. The Lords can delay a bill for up to one year under the Parliament Acts of 1911 and 1949, but they cannot ultimately block legislation that the Commons is determined to pass. The House of Lords acts as a revising chamber, often improving the technical quality of legislation.

The Cabinet is a committee of senior government ministers chosen by the Prime Minister. Cabinet members are responsible for major government departments such as the Treasury, Home Office, and Foreign Office. The Cabinet meets weekly to discuss and agree on government policy. Collective responsibility means all Cabinet members must publicly support decisions made by the Cabinet, even if they privately disagree.
Key roles in the House of Commons:
items: The Prime Minister leads the government and is the leader of the party with the most seats
The Leader of the Opposition leads the second largest party and heads the Shadow Cabinet
The Shadow Cabinet is made up of senior opposition MPs who each 'shadow' a government minister, scrutinising their work and proposing alternative policies
The whips are MPs or Lords appointed by each party to maintain discipline, ensuring members attend important votes and vote according to the party line
Backbenchers are MPs who are not ministers or shadow ministers and sit on the back benches of the Commons chamber

sections.content: How Laws Are Made

A proposed new law is called a bill. Most bills are introduced by the government, but individual MPs can introduce their own proposals known as Private Members' Bills. A bill must pass through several stages in both the House of Commons and the House of Lords before it can become law. These stages include the First Reading (a formal introduction), Second Reading (a debate on the general principles), Committee Stage (detailed line-by-line examination), Report Stage (further consideration and amendments), and Third Reading (a final debate and vote).

After a bill has passed through all stages in both Houses and any disagreements between the two chambers have been resolved, it receives Royal Assent from the monarch. Royal Assent is the formal approval of the monarch, which is required for a bill to become an Act of Parliament and thus become law. In practice, Royal Assent has not been refused since 1708 and is considered a constitutional formality.

Prime Minister's Questions (PMQs) take place every Wednesday at noon when Parliament is sitting. The Leader of the Opposition asks six questions, and other MPs may also ask questions. PMQs is an important mechanism for holding the Prime Minister to account and is broadcast live on television.

Hansard is the official written record of everything said in both Houses of Parliament. It has been published since 1909, though unofficial records date back much earlier. Hansard is publicly available and provides a verbatim account of parliamentary debates, questions, and statements. It is an essential tool for transparency and accountability in the democratic process.
Devolved Governments

Since 1997, some powers have been devolved from the UK Parliament to national parliaments and assemblies in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. The Scottish Parliament sits at Holyrood in Edinburgh, the Welsh Parliament (Senedd Cymru) meets in Cardiff, and the Northern Ireland Assembly is based at Stormont in Belfast. These devolved bodies have the power to make laws on certain matters that affect their own nations.

Powers are divided into reserved powers and devolved powers. Reserved powers are matters that only the UK Parliament at Westminster can legislate on, such as defence, foreign affairs, immigration, and the constitution. Devolved powers are matters that the devolved parliaments and assemblies can legislate on, including health, education, transport, and the environment. The exact split of powers differs between Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.

Despite devolution, the UK Parliament at Westminster remains sovereign. This means that, in theory, it could override or abolish the devolved legislatures, although this would be extremely controversial and is considered politically unthinkable in practice. The Sewel Convention states that Westminster will not normally legislate on devolved matters without the consent of the relevant devolved legislature.
Key facts about the UK Parliament:
items: General elections must be held at a maximum interval of every five years
A by-election is held when a seat in the House of Commons becomes vacant between general elections, for example due to the death or resignation of an MP
The House of Commons chamber has green benches, while the House of Lords chamber has red benches
The government sits on the benches to the right of the Speaker, and the Opposition sits on the benches to the left

sections.content: Devolved powers typically include:
items: Health and social services (e.g., the NHS in Scotland is managed by the Scottish Government)
Education and training
Local government and housing
Agriculture, forestry, and the environment
Some aspects of transport and economic development

sections.content: The Unwritten Constitution

The UK does not have a single written document that serves as its constitution, unlike most other democracies such as the United States or France. Instead, the UK has what is often called an unwritten or uncodified constitution. This means that the rules governing how the country is run come from several different sources, and they have evolved gradually over many centuries rather than being established at a single point in time.

Because the UK constitution is not contained in one document, it can be changed by a simple Act of Parliament. This makes it more flexible than codified constitutions, which typically require special procedures or supermajorities to amend. However, this flexibility also means that constitutional protections can theoretically be altered more easily, which is why conventions and traditions play such an important role.

The sources of the UK constitution include: statute law (Acts of Parliament such as Magna Carta 1215, the Bill of Rights 1689, and the Human Rights Act 1998), common law (law developed through court decisions over centuries), conventions (established practices and customs that are followed but not legally enforceable), and works of authority (influential texts by constitutional scholars such as Walter Bagehot and A.V. Dicey).

Historically, European Union law was also a source of constitutional law in the UK. While the UK was a member of the EU (1973-2020), EU law took precedence over UK law in areas where the EU had competence. Following the UK's departure from the EU (Brexit), EU law has been retained in UK domestic law where applicable, but the UK Parliament now has the power to change or repeal those laws.
The Constitutional Monarchy

The UK is a constitutional monarchy. This means that the country has a monarch (a king or queen) who serves as the head of state, but whose powers are limited by law and convention. The real political power lies with the elected government, led by the Prime Minister. The monarch's role is largely ceremonial, though it remains constitutionally significant. The monarchy provides continuity and stability, standing above the day-to-day politics of government.

King Charles III became King on 8 September 2022, following the death of his mother, Queen Elizabeth II. Queen Elizabeth II reigned from 1952 to 2022, making her the longest-reigning British monarch in history, serving for over 70 years. Her reign saw enormous changes in British society, from the end of the British Empire to the digital age. King Charles III was formally crowned at his coronation ceremony at Westminster Abbey on 6 May 2023.

The monarch holds several important constitutional roles: Head of State of the United Kingdom, Head of the Commonwealth (a voluntary association of 56 nations), and Supreme Governor of the Church of England. The monarch is expected to be politically neutral and does not express political opinions publicly or interfere in party politics.
The Role of the Monarch

The State Opening of Parliament is one of the most important ceremonial duties of the monarch. It takes place at the beginning of each new parliamentary session, usually in the autumn. The monarch travels to the Palace of Westminster and delivers the King's Speech (or Queen's Speech) from the throne in the House of Lords, outlining the government's legislative agenda. The speech is written by the government, not by the monarch.

The monarch gives Royal Assent to all bills that have passed through both Houses of Parliament, formally making them law. The monarch also holds a weekly private audience with the Prime Minister, during which state matters are discussed. These meetings are completely confidential. The monarch has the right to be consulted, to encourage, and to warn, but does not have the power to direct government policy.

The Sovereign Grant replaced the Civil List in 2012 as the mechanism for funding the official duties of the monarch. The Sovereign Grant is a percentage of the profits of the Crown Estate (the monarchy's property portfolio) and is paid annually by the Treasury to cover the costs of the monarch's official duties, staff, and maintenance of royal palaces.

The Prince of Wales is the title traditionally given to the heir to the throne. Prince William, the eldest son of King Charles III, was created Prince of Wales in 2022. His wife, Catherine, holds the title Princess of Wales. The Prince of Wales undertakes official duties and engagements on behalf of the monarch and represents the Royal Family at events both in the UK and abroad.
Devolution: Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland
uk-countries-map.png

Devolution is the transfer of certain powers from the UK Parliament at Westminster to national parliaments and assemblies. This process was a major constitutional development in the late 1990s, carried out under Tony Blair's Labour government. The Scotland Act 1998 established the Scottish Parliament, the Government of Wales Act 1998 created the Welsh Assembly (now called the Senedd Cymru), and the Northern Ireland Act 1998 set up the Northern Ireland Assembly as part of the Good Friday Agreement peace process.

The Scottish Parliament sits at Holyrood in Edinburgh and has 129 Members of the Scottish Parliament (MSPs). It has significant legislative powers, including the ability to set income tax rates and legislate on health, education, justice, and the environment. The Scottish Parliament uses a mixed electoral system combining first-past-the-post and proportional representation.

The Welsh Parliament (Senedd Cymru) is located in Cardiff and has 60 Members of the Senedd (MS). The Northern Ireland Assembly sits at Stormont in Belfast and has 90 Members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs). Both use forms of proportional representation for elections, and both have powers over devolved matters such as health, education, and local government.

The Northern Ireland Assembly operates on a power-sharing principle, requiring cooperation between unionist and nationalist communities. The First Minister and Deputy First Minister must come from different communities, and key decisions require cross-community support. This unique arrangement was established by the Good Friday (Belfast) Agreement of 1998, which helped bring peace after decades of conflict known as the Troubles.
Local Government

Below the national level, the UK has a system of local government. Local authorities, also known as councils, are responsible for providing a range of services in their area, including education, social services, roads, housing, rubbish collection, planning permission, libraries, and leisure facilities. Councils are funded through a combination of central government grants and Council Tax, a local tax based on property values.

Local councillors are elected by residents of the area to represent them on the council. Local elections are held regularly, and any registered voter can stand for election as a councillor. Some areas have a directly elected mayor who leads the council. In major cities such as London, Manchester, and the West Midlands, combined authority mayors have been introduced with powers over transport, housing, and economic development.

The Greater London Authority, headed by the Mayor of London, is responsible for transport (through Transport for London), policing, fire services, and strategic planning across the 32 London boroughs and the City of London. The London Assembly scrutinises the Mayor's activities and decisions.
Key constitutional documents and milestones:
items: Magna Carta (1215) established the principle that everyone, including the king, is subject to the law
The Bill of Rights (1689) confirmed the rights of Parliament and limited the powers of the monarch
The Act of Union (1707) united the parliaments of England and Scotland
The Parliament Acts (1911 and 1949) limited the power of the House of Lords
The Human Rights Act (1998) incorporated the European Convention on Human Rights into UK law

sections.content: Devolved institutions at a glance:
items: Scottish Parliament (Holyrood, Edinburgh): 129 MSPs, established 1999, has tax-varying powers
Welsh Parliament / Senedd Cymru (Cardiff): 60 MS, established 1999, gained primary law-making powers in 2011
Northern Ireland Assembly (Stormont, Belfast): 90 MLAs, established 1998, operates on a power-sharing basis
Each devolved nation has its own government led by a First Minister

sections.content: Representative Democracy in the UK

The UK is a representative democracy, which means that citizens elect people to represent them and make decisions on their behalf. Members of Parliament, local councillors, and members of devolved legislatures are all elected by the people. The right to vote is one of the most important rights of citizens and is central to the democratic process. Voting is not compulsory in the UK, but it is strongly encouraged as a civic duty.

The minimum voting age for UK general elections and most other elections in England and Northern Ireland is 18. However, in Scotland and Wales, the voting age for devolved elections and local elections has been lowered to 16. This means that 16- and 17-year-olds in Scotland can vote in Scottish Parliament elections and local elections, and those in Wales can vote in Senedd elections and local elections.

To be eligible to vote in UK elections, you must be registered on the electoral register. You can register online at www.gov.uk/register-to-vote or by contacting your local council. Registration is a legal requirement, and you can be fined if you do not register when asked to do so. You must register separately; it is not done automatically.
Who Can Vote

Not everyone living in the UK has the right to vote in all elections. To vote in a UK general election, you must be at least 18 years old, be a British citizen, a qualifying Commonwealth citizen, or a citizen of the Irish Republic, be registered on the electoral register, and not be legally excluded from voting. Citizens of EU countries who are not also Commonwealth citizens cannot vote in UK general elections, though they may vote in local elections.

Certain people are not allowed to vote. These include members of the House of Lords (who cannot vote in general elections), people who have been convicted of corrupt or illegal electoral practices, and people who are sectioned under mental health legislation if a court has ruled they lack the capacity to vote. Prisoners serving a custodial sentence are also currently not permitted to vote in UK elections.

The UK uses the first-past-the-post (FPTP) electoral system for general elections. The country is divided into 650 constituencies, and voters in each constituency choose one candidate. The candidate with the most votes wins the seat, regardless of whether they achieve more than 50% of the votes cast. This system tends to produce clear majority governments but can mean that the share of seats does not reflect the overall share of votes nationwide.
Types of Elections

Several types of elections take place in the UK. General elections determine which party forms the UK government and must be held at least every five years. Local elections choose councillors who represent residents on local councils. Elections to the devolved parliaments and assemblies in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland choose members of those legislatures. Police and Crime Commissioner (PCC) elections choose the individuals who oversee local police forces in England and Wales.

By-elections are held when a seat in the House of Commons becomes vacant between general elections, for example if an MP dies, resigns, or is disqualified. Only the affected constituency votes in a by-election. By-elections can be significant political events, sometimes seen as indicators of public opinion on the current government's performance. The devolved legislatures use forms of proportional representation, which allocates seats more closely in proportion to the votes each party receives.

General elections in the UK must be held at a maximum interval of every five years. The Prime Minister can ask the monarch to dissolve Parliament and call a general election earlier than the five-year limit. Before 2022, the Fixed-term Parliaments Act 2011 set the date for general elections, but this was repealed by the Dissolution and Calling of Parliament Act 2022, which restored the monarch's prerogative to dissolve Parliament on the advice of the PM.
History of Voting Rights

The right to vote in the UK was historically very restricted. For centuries, only wealthy men who owned property could vote, which meant the vast majority of the population had no say in how the country was governed. The Great Reform Act of 1832 was the first major step towards expanding the franchise, giving the vote to more middle-class men and redistributing parliamentary seats to better reflect population centres created by the Industrial Revolution.

Further Reform Acts in 1867 and 1884 continued to extend voting rights to more men, including many working-class men. However, women remained completely excluded from voting. The campaign for women's suffrage became one of the most important social movements of the early 20th century. The suffragists campaigned peacefully through petitions and lobbying, while the suffragettes used more militant tactics including protests, hunger strikes, chaining themselves to railings, and civil disobedience.

Emmeline Pankhurst founded the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU) in 1903 to fight for women's right to vote. The suffragettes' motto was 'Deeds, not words.' Their campaign, combined with women's contributions during World War I, helped change public opinion. The Representation of the People Act 1918 gave the vote to women over 30 who met certain property qualifications, and to all men over 21.

Full equal voting rights for women were achieved through the Representation of the People (Equal Franchise) Act 1928, which gave women the vote on the same terms as men, at the age of 21. In 1969, the voting age was lowered from 21 to 18 for all voters by the Representation of the People Act 1969. These progressive expansions of the franchise transformed the UK from an aristocratic oligarchy into a full democracy.
Standing for Election and Political Parties

Any person who is eligible to vote can also stand for election to the House of Commons, provided they are at least 18 years old and are a British citizen, a citizen of the Irish Republic, or a qualifying Commonwealth citizen. Candidates must pay an election deposit (currently £500 for a general election), which is returned if the candidate receives at least 5% of the votes. This is intended to discourage frivolous candidacies.

The UK has a multi-party system, although two parties have historically dominated: the Conservative Party (also known as the Tories) and the Labour Party. The Liberal Democrats are the third largest party in England. Other significant parties include the Scottish National Party (SNP) in Scotland, Plaid Cymru (the Party of Wales) in Wales, and several parties in Northern Ireland including the Democratic Unionist Party (DUP), Sinn Fein, and the Alliance Party.

Independent candidates can also stand for election without belonging to any political party. In local elections, independent candidates are particularly common. Voters can find information about candidates and how to vote from their local council, the Electoral Commission, and through official election materials delivered to their homes before polling day.
Key milestones in the history of UK voting rights:
items: 1832: Great Reform Act gave the vote to more middle-class men and redistributed parliamentary seats
1867 and 1884: Further Reform Acts extended the vote to most working-class men
1903: Emmeline Pankhurst founded the Women's Social and Political Union (WSPU)
1918: Representation of the People Act gave the vote to women over 30 and all men over 21
1928: Equal Franchise Act gave women the same voting rights as men at age 21
1969: Voting age lowered to 18 for all voters

sections.content: Types of elections in the UK:
items: General elections: held every five years (maximum) to choose 650 MPs for the House of Commons using first-past-the-post
Local elections: to choose councillors for local councils, held at regular intervals
Devolved elections: to choose members of the Scottish Parliament, Welsh Senedd, and Northern Ireland Assembly using proportional representation
Police and Crime Commissioner (PCC) elections: to choose the individual who oversees a local police force in England and Wales
By-elections: held when an individual constituency seat becomes vacant between general elections

sections.content: Major UK political parties:
items: Conservative Party (Tories): traditionally centre-right, one of the two largest parties
Labour Party: traditionally centre-left, one of the two largest parties, founded in 1900
Liberal Democrats: centrist party formed in 1988 from a merger of the Liberal Party and the Social Democratic Party
Scottish National Party (SNP): advocates for Scottish independence, dominant in Scotland
Plaid Cymru (Party of Wales): advocates for Welsh self-governance and the Welsh language
Green Party: focuses on environmental and social justice issues

sections.content: Three Separate Legal Systems

The United Kingdom has three separate legal systems, not one. England and Wales share a single legal system, Scotland has its own distinct legal system, and Northern Ireland has a separate legal system as well. Each system has its own courts, legal traditions, and in some cases different laws. This arrangement reflects the historical development of the UK as a union of previously independent nations.

The legal system of England and Wales is based on two main sources of law: common law and statute law. Common law has developed over centuries through the decisions of judges in court cases (also known as case law or precedent). Statute law consists of Acts of Parliament and secondary legislation. When a court makes a decision in a case, that decision can become a precedent that lower courts must follow in similar future cases.

There is an important distinction between criminal law and civil law. Criminal law deals with offences against the state or society, such as theft, assault, and murder. The state prosecutes criminal cases and punishments can include fines, community sentences, and imprisonment. Civil law deals with disputes between individuals or organisations, such as contract disputes, family matters, and negligence claims.
Criminal Courts in England and Wales

Criminal cases in England and Wales are heard in two types of court depending on the seriousness of the offence. Magistrates' Courts deal with less serious offences (known as summary offences), such as minor theft, motoring offences, and minor assault. Magistrates are usually volunteers from the local community (also called Justices of the Peace) who are not legally qualified. They are advised on points of law by a legally qualified clerk. Some Magistrates' Courts have a single District Judge who sits alone.

More serious criminal offences (known as indictable offences), such as murder, rape, and serious fraud, are tried in the Crown Court before a judge and a jury of 12 members of the public. The jury listens to all the evidence presented by the prosecution and the defence and decides whether the defendant is guilty or not guilty. The judge directs the jury on matters of law and, if the defendant is found guilty, decides the appropriate sentence.

A jury in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland consists of 12 randomly selected members of the public between the ages of 18 and 76. Jury service is a legal duty, and you can be fined if you do not attend when summoned. Jurors must be registered on the electoral roll and must not have serious criminal convictions. In Scotland, a jury in a criminal trial consists of 15 members.
Civil Courts and the Higher Courts

Civil cases in England and Wales are usually dealt with in County Courts for less serious matters or the High Court for more complex or high-value cases. The High Court is divided into three divisions: the Queen's Bench Division (now King's Bench Division), which deals with contract and tort (negligence) cases; the Chancery Division, which deals with business, property, and probate cases; and the Family Division, which deals with family law matters such as divorce and child custody.

Small claims are civil cases involving relatively small amounts of money (generally under £10,000). These are dealt with through a simplified procedure in the County Court, designed to be accessible to people without legal representation. The small claims track is less formal than other court proceedings and is intended to resolve disputes quickly and cheaply.

Above the County Courts and Crown Courts sits the Court of Appeal, which hears appeals from lower courts. The Court of Appeal has two divisions: the Criminal Division (hearing appeals from the Crown Court) and the Civil Division (hearing appeals from the County Courts and the High Court). The highest court in the UK is the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom, which was established in 2009 and replaced the judicial function that had previously been performed by the House of Lords.

The Supreme Court of the United Kingdom is the final court of appeal for all civil cases in the UK and for criminal cases in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. It was created by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005 and began work on 1 October 2009. The Supreme Court sits in the former Middlesex Guildhall on Parliament Square in London. It has 12 Justices, led by the President of the Supreme Court.
The Scottish Legal System

Scotland's legal system is distinct from that of England and Wales and has its own traditions, courts, and procedures. Criminal cases in Scotland are heard in Justice of the Peace Courts (for minor offences), Sheriff Courts (for most criminal and civil cases), and the High Court of Justiciary (for the most serious crimes such as murder and treason). The High Court of Justiciary is the supreme criminal court in Scotland, and there is no appeal from it to the UK Supreme Court on criminal matters.

The highest civil court in Scotland is the Court of Session, which sits in Edinburgh. Civil cases in Scotland may be appealed from the Court of Session to the UK Supreme Court. A distinctive feature of the Scottish criminal justice system is the 'not proven' verdict. In addition to 'guilty' and 'not guilty', a Scottish jury can return a verdict of 'not proven', which results in the acquittal of the accused. This unique third verdict has no equivalent elsewhere in the UK.

The Scottish legal system has a unique 'not proven' verdict, sometimes called the 'Scottish verdict.' It has the same legal effect as 'not guilty' in that the accused is acquitted and cannot be retried for the same offence. However, it is sometimes seen as meaning 'not guilty, but don't do it again.' Scottish juries also have 15 members rather than the 12 used in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland.
Fundamental Legal Principles and Access to Justice

Several fundamental principles underpin the UK legal system. The presumption of innocence means that a person is considered innocent until proven guilty in a court of law. The burden of proof in criminal cases lies with the prosecution, which must prove the defendant's guilt beyond reasonable doubt. Everyone has the right to a fair trial, the right to be represented by a lawyer, and the right to remain silent and not incriminate themselves.

The principle of double jeopardy means that a person cannot be tried twice for the same offence once they have been acquitted or convicted. However, since the Criminal Justice Act 2003, this rule has been modified in England and Wales to allow a retrial in certain serious cases (such as murder) if compelling new evidence emerges. The independence of the judiciary is another essential principle, meaning judges must be free from political interference and make decisions based solely on the law and the evidence.

The police in the UK are independent of the government and are accountable to the law. They must follow strict rules about the treatment of suspects, including the right to have a lawyer present during questioning. In England and Wales, Police and Crime Commissioners (PCCs) are directly elected by the public to hold the local police force to account and set policing priorities. PCCs were introduced in 2012 to strengthen democratic accountability of policing.

Legal aid is a system of government funding to help people who cannot afford legal representation. It is available for both criminal and civil cases, though eligibility depends on the nature of the case and the applicant's financial circumstances. Tribunals are specialist judicial bodies that deal with particular types of disputes, such as employment tribunals (hearing cases about unfair dismissal, discrimination, and wages) and immigration tribunals (hearing appeals against immigration and asylum decisions).

Youth justice in the UK provides a separate system for dealing with young offenders.
