If your GP surgery is closed, or if you need medical advice but it is not an emergency, you should call NHS 111. The NHS 111 service is available 24 hours a day, 7 days a week, and is staffed by trained advisers who can direct you to the most appropriate service.

Your GP (General Practitioner) is your first point of contact for non-emergency health issues. NHS 111 provides non-emergency medical advice 24/7. For life-threatening emergencies, call 999 or go to the nearest Accident and Emergency (A&E) department.

NHS walk-in centres and urgent care centres are available in many areas and provide treatment for minor illnesses and injuries without the need for an appointment. Pharmacies (chemists) are also an important part of the primary care system and can provide advice on common health problems, sell over-the-counter medicines, and dispense prescriptions.
Key NHS telephone numbers and services:
items: 999: Emergency services (police, fire, ambulance) for life-threatening situations
112: Alternative emergency number that works across the EU and from mobile phones
111: NHS non-emergency medical helpline, available 24 hours a day
GP surgery: Your first point of contact for non-emergency health concerns
NHS walk-in centres: For minor injuries and illnesses without an appointment
Pharmacies: Advice on common health problems and dispensing prescriptions

sections.content: Hospital Care and Specialist Services

Hospitals provide emergency care through Accident and Emergency (A&E) departments, as well as planned (elective) surgery, specialist treatment, and diagnostic services. If you need specialist care, your GP will usually refer you to a hospital consultant. A&E departments are for genuine emergencies such as loss of consciousness, severe bleeding, suspected heart attacks, strokes, or serious injuries.

Midwives provide care to women during pregnancy, labour, and after birth. Health visitors support families with young children, offering advice on child development, feeding, and immunisations. Mental health services are available through the NHS, and your GP can refer you for counselling, therapy, or specialist psychiatric care. The NHS also provides screening programmes for conditions such as cancer, and vaccination programmes to protect against diseases.

The NHS provides midwives for maternity care, health visitors for families with young children, mental health services, and screening and vaccination programmes. GPs refer patients to hospital specialists when more advanced treatment is needed.
Prescription Charges and Exemptions

While most NHS treatment is free, there are charges for prescriptions in England. However, prescriptions are free in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. In England, a fixed charge applies per item prescribed, regardless of the actual cost of the medicine. People who need regular prescriptions can save money by purchasing a Prescription Prepayment Certificate (PPC).

Many groups of people are exempt from prescription charges in England. These include children under 16 (or under 19 if in full-time education), people aged 60 and over, pregnant women and those who have had a baby in the previous 12 months, people on certain benefits or with low incomes, and those with certain medical conditions. NHS dental treatment and eye tests also carry charges, but exemptions apply to similar groups.

Prescriptions are free in Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland. In England, charges apply but exemptions exist for children under 16, people over 60, pregnant women, those on low incomes, and people with certain medical conditions.
Groups exempt from prescription charges in England:
items: Children under 16 (or under 19 in full-time education)
People aged 60 and over
Pregnant women and women who have had a baby in the previous 12 months
People receiving certain benefits or on low incomes (with a valid HC2 certificate)
People with certain medical conditions such as diabetes, epilepsy, or cancer
War pensioners (for prescriptions related to their disability)

sections.content: Dental Care, Opticians, and Organ Donation

NHS dental care is available, but you may need to find an NHS dentist who is accepting new patients, as not all dentists offer NHS treatment. NHS dental treatment is divided into bands, with different charges for different types of treatment. Dental check-ups, fillings, root canals, and more complex treatments each fall into different cost bands. As with prescriptions, certain groups are exempt from dental charges.

Eye tests are available from opticians (optometrists) and are free for certain groups including children under 16, people aged 60 and over, people receiving certain benefits, and those diagnosed with or at risk of certain eye conditions such as glaucoma. If you need glasses or contact lenses, you will generally need to pay for them, although NHS vouchers are available for some groups to help with the cost.

NHS dental care has charges divided into bands. Eye tests are free for children, people over 60, and those on certain benefits. England, Wales, and Scotland operate an opt-out organ donation system (also called 'deemed consent' or 'Max and Keira's Law' in England), meaning all adults are considered potential donors unless they have specifically opted out. Northern Ireland has introduced a similar system. The NHS Organ Donor Register allows people to record their wishes.

The NHS Constitution sets out the rights of patients, the pledges the NHS makes, and the responsibilities of patients, staff, and the public. It establishes the principles and values of the NHS and was first published in 2009.
Other important NHS services:
items: NHS walk-in centres provide treatment for minor conditions without appointments
Pharmacies offer health advice and can sell over-the-counter medications
Vaccination programmes protect children and adults against infectious diseases
Screening programmes help detect conditions such as breast cancer, cervical cancer, and bowel cancer early
Blood donation is organised by NHS Blood and Transplant, and donors are always needed

sections.content: Compulsory Education and School Structure

Education is compulsory for all children in England between the ages of 5 and 18. In Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland, compulsory schooling covers ages 5 to 16, after which young people may continue in education, take up an apprenticeship, or enter training. Children in England typically start reception class at age 4 or 5, which serves as an introduction to formal schooling.

The school system is divided into primary school (ages 5 to 11) and secondary school (ages 11 to 16, or 18 if the school has a sixth form). Primary education covers Key Stage 1 (ages 5-7) and Key Stage 2 (ages 7-11). Secondary education covers Key Stage 3 (ages 11-14) and Key Stage 4 (ages 14-16), during which students prepare for their GCSE examinations. The National Curriculum applies to all state schools in England and sets out the subjects and standards that children should be taught at each Key Stage, with core subjects including English, mathematics, and science.

In England, education is compulsory from age 5 to 18. Children attend primary school from ages 5 to 11 and secondary school from ages 11 to 16 or 18. The National Curriculum sets out what must be taught in state schools in England.
Types of Schools

State schools are funded by the government and are free to attend. They include community schools (run by the local authority), academies (independently managed but state-funded), free schools (set up by parents, teachers, charities, or businesses, funded by the government but not run by the local authority), grammar schools (which select pupils based on academic ability through an entrance exam, often called the 11-plus), and faith schools (associated with a particular religion).

Comprehensive schools accept pupils of all abilities and are the most common type of secondary school in the UK. Independent schools (also known as private or public schools) charge fees and do not have to follow the National Curriculum. Some of the most well-known independent schools include Eton College, Harrow, and Winchester. These schools often have their own entrance examinations and selection processes.

State schools are free and funded by the government. Types include academies, free schools, grammar schools (which select by ability), faith schools, and comprehensive schools. Independent (private) schools charge fees and do not have to follow the National Curriculum.
Types of schools in the UK:
items: Community schools: Run by the local authority, follow the National Curriculum
Academies: State-funded but independently managed, more freedom over curriculum
Free schools: Set up by groups such as parents, teachers, or charities; state-funded
Grammar schools: Select pupils by academic ability through entrance examinations
Faith schools: Associated with a religion; can set their own admissions criteria
Comprehensive schools: Accept pupils of all abilities, the most common type
Independent (private) schools: Charge tuition fees, not required to follow the National Curriculum

sections.content: Qualifications: GCSEs, A-Levels, and Scottish Equivalents

At the end of Key Stage 4, usually at age 16, students in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland take GCSE (General Certificate of Secondary Education) examinations. GCSEs are taken in a range of subjects, and the results help determine what students do next. In England, GCSEs are graded on a 9-1 scale, with 9 being the highest. Students must achieve a grade 4 or above in English and mathematics.

After GCSEs, students who wish to continue academic study typically take A-levels (Advanced Level qualifications) over two years in a school sixth form or at a sixth form college. A-levels are the main qualification used for university entry. Students usually study three or four subjects at A-level. Results are graded from A* to E.

In England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, students take GCSEs at age 16 and may continue to A-levels at age 18. In Scotland, the equivalent qualifications are National 5s (taken at around age 16) and Highers (taken at around age 17), which are the main qualifications for university entry in Scotland. Advanced Highers, taken at age 18, are broadly equivalent to A-levels.
Further and Higher Education

Further education colleges offer a wide range of courses for students aged 16 and over, including vocational qualifications, BTECs, apprenticeships, and A-levels. Apprenticeships combine practical training in a job with study, and apprentices earn a wage while they learn. They are available at different levels, from intermediate to degree-level apprenticeships.

The UK has a world-renowned higher education system. The University of Oxford was founded in the 12th century and is the oldest university in the English-speaking world. The University of Cambridge was founded in the 13th century. Together they are known as 'Oxbridge.' The University of St Andrews, founded in 1413, is the oldest university in Scotland. Other leading universities include Edinburgh, University College London, Imperial College London, and the London School of Economics.

The University of Oxford (founded in the 12th century) is the oldest English-speaking university in the world. Cambridge was founded in the 13th century. St Andrews (founded 1413) is the oldest university in Scotland. Students apply through UCAS (Universities and Colleges Admissions Service).

Students apply to UK universities through UCAS (Universities and Colleges Admissions Service). Tuition fees for undergraduate courses in England can be up to 9,250 pounds per year. Students from Scotland studying at Scottish universities do not pay tuition fees. Student loans are available from the government to cover tuition fees and living costs, and repayments are income-contingent, meaning graduates only begin to repay once they earn above a certain threshold.
Key facts about higher education:
items: University of Oxford: Founded in the 12th century, oldest English-speaking university
University of Cambridge: Founded in the 13th century
University of St Andrews: Founded in 1413, oldest in Scotland
UCAS: The central system for applying to universities in the UK
Tuition fees in England: Up to 9,250 pounds per year for undergraduates
Scottish students studying in Scotland: Do not pay tuition fees
Student loans: Available for tuition and living costs; repaid only above an income threshold

sections.content: Other Education Matters

Parents have the legal right to educate their children at home (home education or home schooling). They do not need permission from the local authority, but they must ensure that the education provided is suitable for the child's age, ability, and aptitude. Home-educated children do not have to follow the National Curriculum or take national tests.

Ofsted (the Office for Standards in Education, Children's Services and Skills) is the body responsible for inspecting schools in England and rating them as outstanding, good, requires improvement, or inadequate. Schools are also supported by school governors, who are volunteers from the local community, parents, and staff who help oversee the school's management and direction. Parent-teacher associations (PTAs) help raise funds and organise events.

Parents have the legal right to educate their children at home. Ofsted inspects schools in England and rates their quality. ESOL (English for Speakers of Other Languages) courses are widely available for adults who need to improve their English language skills.

Adult education is widely available throughout the UK, with courses offered by further education colleges, local authorities, and community organisations. ESOL (English for Speakers of Other Languages) courses are available for adults who need to improve their English. Many of these courses are free or subsidised. Learning English is important for settling into life in the UK, accessing services, finding employment, and participating in community life.
Education oversight and support:
items: Ofsted: Inspects and rates schools in England (outstanding, good, requires improvement, inadequate)
School governors: Volunteers who help oversee school management and strategy
Parent-teacher associations (PTAs): Raise funds and organise events for schools
Home education: Legal right of parents; no need to follow the National Curriculum
ESOL courses: Help adults learn English; often free or subsidised

sections.content: Buying and Renting a Home

Most people in the UK either buy or rent their home. Buying a home usually involves obtaining a mortgage (a loan from a bank or building society), paying a deposit (typically 5-20% of the property price), hiring a solicitor or conveyancer to handle the legal aspects, and arranging a survey to check the condition of the property. Stamp Duty Land Tax (in England and Northern Ireland) is payable on properties above a certain value.

Renting from a private landlord is common, especially for younger people and those new to an area. The most common type of rental agreement in England and Wales is an assured shorthold tenancy (AST). Tenants' deposits must be placed in a government-approved deposit protection scheme. Landlords are responsible for maintaining the structure and exterior of the property, ensuring gas and electrical safety, and providing an Energy Performance Certificate.

When buying a home, you typically need a mortgage, a deposit, a solicitor, and a survey. Stamp Duty Land Tax applies to property purchases above a certain value in England and Northern Ireland. Renters in England and Wales usually have an assured shorthold tenancy, and deposits must be protected in a government-approved scheme.

Tenants have important legal rights, including the right to live in a property that is safe and in good repair, protection from unfair eviction, and the right to have their deposit returned at the end of the tenancy (subject to any valid deductions). Landlords must follow a legal process to evict tenants, and tenants can challenge unfair evictions through the courts. Tenants are usually responsible for paying rent on time, keeping the property in reasonable condition, and paying utility bills such as gas, electricity, and water.
Social Housing and Council Tax

Social housing is provided by local councils (council housing) and housing associations at lower rents than the private sector. There are usually waiting lists for social housing, and priority is given based on need, such as families with children, people with disabilities, or those at risk of homelessness. Local councils have a duty to help people who are homeless or at risk of becoming homeless.

Council Tax is a local tax collected by local authorities (councils) to pay for local services such as schools, roads, rubbish collection, street cleaning, libraries, police, and fire services. The amount of Council Tax you pay depends on the value of your property, which is placed in one of eight bands (A to H in England and Scotland, A to I in Wales). Band A is the lowest and Band H (or I) is the highest.

Council Tax is based on the value of your property and is divided into bands A to H. Single occupants receive a 25% discount. Full-time students are exempt from Council Tax. Social housing is provided by local councils and housing associations at below-market rents.
Key housing facts:
items: Assured shorthold tenancy (AST): The most common type of rental agreement in England and Wales
Deposit protection: Landlords must place tenant deposits in a government-approved scheme
Council Tax bands: Properties are placed in bands A-H based on value; Band A is the lowest
Council Tax discounts: 25% discount for single occupants; full-time students are exempt
Social housing: Provided by local councils and housing associations; usually has waiting lists
Homelessness: Local councils have a legal duty to help those who are homeless or at risk

sections.content: Employment: National Insurance and Working Rights

Everyone who works in the UK needs a National Insurance (NI) number. This is a unique personal number used to track National Insurance contributions and tax. You can apply for a National Insurance number by calling the National Insurance number application line or applying online. You will usually need to attend an interview to verify your identity. Your NI number ensures that your contributions are properly recorded for benefits such as the State Pension.

Most employees in the UK are paid through the PAYE (Pay As You Earn) system, which means their employer deducts income tax and National Insurance contributions from their wages before they are paid. Self-employed people must register with HMRC (HM Revenue and Customs) and complete a self-assessment tax return each year to report their income and pay any tax owed.

Everyone who works in the UK needs a National Insurance (NI) number. Most employees pay tax through PAYE (Pay As You Earn). Self-employed people must register with HMRC and file a self-assessment tax return annually.

The UK has a National Minimum Wage for workers aged under 23 and a National Living Wage for workers aged 23 and over. The rates are reviewed and usually increased every year. Employers who pay less than the minimum wage are breaking the law. Workers are entitled to receive at least the minimum wage for every hour they work, regardless of whether they are full-time, part-time, or on a zero-hours contract.
Employment Rights and Protections

UK employment law provides extensive protections for workers. All employees have the right to a written statement of employment (setting out terms and conditions), a minimum of 28 days paid holiday per year (which can include bank holidays), Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) if they are too ill to work, and protection from unfair dismissal after a qualifying period of employment.

Maternity leave allows women to take up to 52 weeks off work when they have a baby, with Statutory Maternity Pay for up to 39 weeks. Partners are entitled to paternity leave, usually one or two weeks. Shared parental leave allows parents to share up to 50 weeks of leave. Employees also have the right to request flexible working arrangements.

Employees are entitled to 28 days paid holiday per year, Statutory Sick Pay (SSP), up to 52 weeks maternity leave, and protection from unfair dismissal. The Working Time Directive limits the working week to 48 hours on average, although workers can choose to opt out.

The Working Time Directive limits the average working week to 48 hours, although individual workers can choose to opt out of this limit if they wish. Employees who believe their rights have been violated can take their case to an employment tribunal. Trade unions represent workers in negotiations with employers over pay and conditions, and employees have the right to join a trade union.
Key employment rights in the UK:
items: Written statement of employment setting out terms and conditions
National Minimum Wage / National Living Wage (reviewed annually)
28 days paid holiday per year (including bank holidays)
Statutory Sick Pay (SSP) when too ill to work
Maternity leave (up to 52 weeks) and paternity leave (1-2 weeks)
Protection from unfair dismissal and redundancy rights
Right to join a trade union
Working Time Directive: 48-hour average working week (with opt-out option)
Health and safety protections in the workplace
Protection from discrimination based on protected characteristics

sections.content: The Tax System

Income tax in the UK is collected by HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC). Everyone has a personal allowance, which is the amount of income you can earn before you start paying tax. Income above the personal allowance is taxed at different rates depending on how much you earn: the basic rate, the higher rate, and the additional rate. National Insurance contributions (NICs) are paid by both employees and employers and help fund the State Pension, the NHS, and other benefits. Value Added Tax (VAT) is charged at 20% on most goods and services, although some items such as children's clothing and most food are zero-rated or exempt.

Income tax is collected by HMRC. Everyone has a tax-free personal allowance. National Insurance contributions fund the State Pension and NHS. VAT is charged at 20% on most goods and services. Self-employed people must file a self-assessment tax return.
Key facts about the UK tax system:
items: HMRC (HM Revenue and Customs) collects income tax and National Insurance
Personal allowance: The amount you can earn before paying income tax
PAYE: System where employers deduct tax and NI from wages automatically
Self-assessment: Tax return filed by self-employed people and others with untaxed income
VAT: Value Added Tax at 20% on most goods and services
National Insurance contributions: Fund the State Pension, NHS, and benefits

sections.content: Driving in the UK

The minimum age to drive a car in the UK is 17. To learn to drive, you must first obtain a provisional driving licence from the DVLA (Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency). With a provisional licence, you can drive while supervised by a qualified driver who is at least 21 years old and has held a full licence for at least three years. Learner drivers must display L plates (or D plates in Wales) on the car.

To gain a full driving licence, you must pass both a theory test (which includes a multiple-choice section and a hazard perception test) and a practical driving test. Once you have a full licence, you can drive unsupervised. People in the UK drive on the left-hand side of the road, which is different from most other countries in the world. The steering wheel is on the right-hand side of the car.
The minimum driving age is 17. You must obtain a provisional licence from the DVLA, then pass a theory test and a practical driving test. People in the UK drive on the left-hand side of the road.

All vehicles used on public roads must have valid insurance. The minimum legal requirement is third-party insurance, which covers damage or injury you cause to other people and their property. Vehicles over three years old must have a valid MOT test certificate, which confirms the vehicle meets minimum road safety and environmental standards. Vehicle Excise Duty (road tax) must also be paid, and the amount depends on the vehicle's CO2 emissions.
Driving Laws and Safety

Drink-driving is a serious offence in the UK. The legal limit for alcohol in the blood is 80 milligrams per 100 millilitres of blood in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland. In Scotland, the limit is lower at 50 milligrams per 100 millilitres. However, the safest advice is not to drink any alcohol at all if you plan to drive. Penalties for drink-driving can include a driving ban, a fine, and imprisonment.

It is illegal to use a hand-held mobile phone while driving, including when stopped at traffic lights or in traffic. Using a hands-free device is permitted but still discouraged. All occupants of a car must wear a seatbelt, and children must use an appropriate child car seat until they are 12 years old or 135cm tall. Drivers who break traffic laws may receive penalty points on their licence, and accumulating 12 or more points within three years can result in disqualification from driving.

The drink-driving limit is 80mg per 100ml of blood in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, and 50mg in Scotland. It is illegal to use a hand-held mobile phone while driving. All vehicle occupants must wear seatbelts. Accumulating 12 penalty points can result in disqualification.
Essential driving requirements in the UK:
items: Minimum driving age: 17 years old
Provisional licence from DVLA required before learning to drive
Must pass theory test and practical driving test for a full licence
Drive on the LEFT side of the road
MOT test required annually for vehicles over 3 years old
Vehicle Excise Duty (road tax) must be paid
Car insurance is compulsory (third-party minimum)
Seatbelts must be worn by all occupants
Hand-held mobile phone use while driving is illegal
Drink-driving limits: 80mg/100ml blood (England/Wales/NI), 50mg/100ml (Scotland)

sections.content: Public Transport and Travel

The UK has an extensive public transport network. Buses operate in towns and cities across the country and are often the most affordable form of public transport. Trains connect major cities and towns through the National Rail network. London has the oldest underground railway system in the world, known as the Tube or London Underground, which opened in 1863. Several cities also have tram systems.

In London, public transport can be paid for using an Oyster card (a rechargeable smart card) or by using contactless payment with a bank card or mobile device, which is often cheaper than buying individual paper tickets. The Eurostar high-speed train service connects London (St Pancras International) with Paris and Brussels through the Channel Tunnel. The UK also has several major airports, including Heathrow (one of the busiest in the world), Gatwick, Manchester, Stansted, and Edinburgh.

The London Underground (the Tube) is the oldest underground railway in the world, opened in 1863. The Oyster card and contactless payment are used for public transport in London. Eurostar connects London to Paris and Brussels through the Channel Tunnel. Licensed taxis can be hailed on the street, while private hire vehicles must be pre-booked.
UK transport options:
items: Buses: Widely available in towns and cities; often the most affordable option
Trains: National Rail connects major cities and towns across the UK
London Underground (the Tube): The oldest underground railway in the world
Trams: Available in cities such as Manchester, Sheffield, Nottingham, and Croydon
Oyster card and contactless: Used for cheaper travel on London public transport
Eurostar: High-speed train from London St Pancras to Paris and Brussels
Taxis: Licensed taxis can be hailed; private hire vehicles must be pre-booked
Airports: Heathrow, Gatwick, Manchester, Stansted, Edinburgh, and others

sections.content: Community Participation and Volunteering

Getting involved in community life is an important part of living in the UK. Volunteering is widespread and highly valued, with opportunities including helping at local charities, food banks, hospitals, schools, sports clubs, and community centres. Neighbourhood Watch is a community-led scheme where residents work together with the local police to reduce crime and improve safety in their area. Local councils and councillors represent residents on issues such as planning, housing, education, and local services, and council meetings are open to the public.

Volunteering is highly valued in the UK and opportunities include helping at charities, schools, hospitals, and community centres. Neighbourhood Watch schemes bring residents and police together to reduce crime. Local councillors represent residents and council meetings are open to the public.

Citizens can serve their community in more formal roles. Becoming a school governor involves volunteering to help oversee a school's management, budget, and curriculum. Serving as a magistrate means sitting in court to hear less serious criminal cases and some civil matters. Special constables are volunteer police officers who have the same powers as regular police officers. All of these roles are unpaid and open to members of the public.
Essential Services, Advice, and Practical Information

The Citizens Advice Bureau (Citizens Advice) provides free, confidential, independent advice on a wide range of matters including legal issues, financial problems, housing, employment, immigration, and consumer rights. Services are available in person, by phone, and online. Public libraries are free to use and provide access to books, computers, the internet, newspapers, and community events, and many also offer children's activities, reading groups, and language classes.

The National Trust and English Heritage protect and maintain historic buildings, monuments, gardens, and areas of natural beauty across England, Wales, and Northern Ireland, while the National Trust for Scotland performs a similar role in Scotland. These organisations are partly funded by membership subscriptions and entrance fees, and many of their properties are open to the public.

Citizens Advice provides free advice on legal, financial, housing, and employment matters. Public libraries offer free access to books, computers, and the internet. The National Trust and English Heritage protect historic buildings and natural sites.

All births, marriages, and deaths must be registered in the UK. Births must be registered within 42 days (in England and Wales) or 21 days (in Scotland). Marriages must follow specific legal procedures and can take place in register offices, approved venues, or places of worship. Deaths must be registered within five days (in England and Wales) or eight days (in Scotland). Registration is handled by the local register office.

A TV licence is required by law to watch or record live television programmes on any channel, or to download or watch BBC programmes on iPlayer. The licence fee is used to fund the BBC, and failure to have a TV licence when one is required can result in a fine. Blood donation is organised by NHS Blood and Transplant, and donors are always needed.
Important telephone numbers and helplines:
items: 999 or 112: Emergency services (police, fire, ambulance, coastguard)
101: Non-emergency police telephone number
111: NHS non-emergency medical helpline (24/7)
0800 1111: Childline (free, confidential helpline for children and young people)
Citizens Advice: Free advice on legal, financial, housing, and employment issues
Samaritans (116 123): Free emotional support helpline available 24/7

sections.content: Practical information for everyday life:
items: Register births within 42 days in England/Wales, 21 days in Scotland
Register deaths within 5 days in England/Wales, 8 days in Scotland
TV licence is required to watch live TV or use BBC iPlayer
Blood donation: Contact NHS Blood and Transplant to become a donor
Places of worship: Churches, mosques, temples, synagogues, and gurdwaras are found across the UK
Community centres: Offer activities, classes, and meeting spaces for local residents
